standard_description:
Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100. (Students who can generate equivalent fractions can develop strategies for adding fractions with unlike denominators in general. But addition and subtraction with unlike denominators in general is not a requirement at this grade.) For example, express 3/10 as 30/100, and add 3/10 + 4/100 = 34/100.
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.
subject: Math

Alignments to Content Standards:  4.NF.C.5
Task
Explain why

IM Commentary
The explanation below is a teacher-level explanation. Students may not articulate every
detail, but the basic idea for a case like the one shown here is that when you have
equivalent fractions, you have just cut the pieces that represent the fraction into more
but smaller pieces. Explaining fraction equivalences at higher grades can be a bit more
involved (e.g. 
), but it can always be framed as subdividing the same quantity in
different ways. 
Note that there is a subtlety with the idea of "equal pieces." It is easiest to show this
with a drawing where the "pieces" are represented by congruent figures; later students
will deal with figures that are not congruent but have equal area. The figures shown in
the solution show congruent pieces, avoiding this complexity.

khan_exercises.title: Equivalent expressions with common denominators (denominators 10 & 100)
khan_exercises.description: Rewrite a fraction addition problem so the fractions have common denominators.  All items have denominators of either 10 or 100.

items.question_content:
**Which expression is equivalent to $\dfrac7{10}+ \dfrac{13}{100}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To add fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

So, we are looking for an equivalent expression that has common denominators.

Let's change $\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$ to an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$. 


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}=\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}$

So, we can replace $\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$ with $\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}$ in our expression.

$\blueD{\dfrac7{10}}+ \dfrac{13}{100}=\blueD{\dfrac{70}{100}}+ \dfrac{13}{100}$

items.question_content:
**Match each expression to the equivalent sum.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints: To add fractions, we need them to have the same denominator.

###Expression $\dfrac{3}{10}+\dfrac{7}{10}$

The fractions have a common denominators, so we are ready to add. 

$\dfrac{3}{10}+\dfrac{7}{10}=\dfrac{10}{10}$

###Expression $\dfrac{3}{100}+\dfrac{7}{10}$ 

$\dfrac{3}{100}$ and $\dfrac{7}{10}$ have different denominators, so we cannot add yet. 

Let's rewrite  $\dfrac{7}{10}$ as an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$.  

$\dfrac{7}{10}=\dfrac{7 \times 10}{10 \times 10} = \dfrac{70}{100}$

Now, we can add.

$\dfrac{3}{100}+\dfrac{7}{10}=\dfrac{3}{100}+\dfrac{70}{100}=\dfrac{73}{100}$

###Expression $\dfrac{3}{10}+\dfrac{7}{100}$ 

$\dfrac{3}{10}$ and $\dfrac{7}{100}$ have different denominators, so we cannot add yet. 

Let's rewrite  $\dfrac{3}{10}$ as an equivalent fraction with a denominator of $100$.  

$\dfrac{3}{10}=\dfrac{3 \times 10}{10 \times 10} = \dfrac{30}{100}$

Now, we can add.

$\dfrac{3}{10}+\dfrac{7}{100}=\dfrac{30}{100}+\dfrac{7}{100}=\dfrac{37}{100}$

Here is the completed solution.

Expression | Sum
- | :- 
$\dfrac{3}{10}+\dfrac{7}{10}$| $\dfrac{10}{10}$
$\dfrac{3}{100}+\dfrac{7}{10}$ | $\dfrac{73}{100}$
$\dfrac{3}{10}+\dfrac{7}{100}$ | $\dfrac{37}{100}$
